(formerly **Relude Oy** and **Rovio Mobile Oy**) is a Finnish [video game developer](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer") based in [Espoo](/wiki/Espoo "Espoo"). Founded in 2003 by [Helsinki University of Technology](/wiki/Helsinki_University_of_Technology "Helsinki University of Technology") students Niklas Hed, Jarno Väkeväinen and Kim Dikert, the company is best known for the *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")* franchise. The company currently operates studios in Barcelona, Toronto, Espoo, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), and [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), with former offices in Montreal. The company's success has helped to establish Finland as a leading player in the mobile game industry and has helped to create a thriving ecosystem for game development in the country.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In August 2023, [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") purchased Rovio for US$776 million and it was made a subsidiary of the Sega Europe division.[[3]](#cite_note-Reid-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Rovio Entertainment Oy** (formerly **Relude Oy** and **Rovio Mobile Oy**) is a Finnish [video game developer](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer") based in [Espoo](/wiki/Espoo "Espoo"). Founded in 2003 by [Helsinki University of Technology](/wiki/Helsinki_University_of_Technology "Helsinki University of Technology") students Niklas Hed, Jarno Väkeväinen and Kim Dikert, the company is best known for the *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")* franchise. The company currently operates studios in Barcelona, Toronto, Espoo, [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), and [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), with former offices in Montreal. The company's success has helped to establish Finland as a leading player in the mobile game industry and has helped to create a thriving ecosystem for game development in the country.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In August 2023, [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") purchased Rovio for US$776 million and it was made a subsidiary of the Sega Europe division.[[3]](#cite_note-Reid-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rovio_Entertainment&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 2003–2009: early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rovio_Entertainment&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2003–2009: early years")]

In 2003, three students from the [Helsinki University of Technology](/wiki/Helsinki_University_of_Technology "Helsinki University of Technology"), Niklas Hed, Jarno Väkeväinen and Kim Dikert, participated in a mobile game development competition at the [Assembly demo party](/wiki/Assembly_demo_party "Assembly demo party") sponsored by [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") and [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"). A victory with a mobile game called *King of the Cabbage World* led the trio to set up their own company, Relude. *King of the Cabbage World* was sold to Sumea, and renamed to *Mole War*, which became one of the first commercial real-time multiplayer mobile games. In January 2005, Relude received its first round of investment from a [business angel](/wiki/Angel_investor "Angel investor"), and the company changed its name to Rovio Mobile, where "rovio" translates from [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language") as "[pyre](/wiki/Pyre "Pyre")".[[5]](#cite_note-venturebeat.com-5)

In 2009, the board gave Mikael Hed the job of CEO. In December 2009, Rovio released *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds_(video_game) "Angry Birds (video game)")*, its 52nd game, a puzzle game where a bird is flung at pigs using a slingshot for the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"); it reached the No. 1 spot in the [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)") paid apps chart after six months, and remained charted for months after.[[5]](#cite_note-venturebeat.com-5)

### 2010–2014: international success

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rovio_Entertainment&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2010–2014: international success")]

In March 2011, Rovio raised $42 million in venture capital funding from Accel Partners, Atomico and Felicis Ventures.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In July 2011, the company changed its name to Rovio Entertainment.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) In June 2011, the company hired [David Maisel](/wiki/David_Maisel "David Maisel") to lead their [*Angry Birds* movie](/wiki/The_Angry_Birds_Movie "The Angry Birds Movie") production.[[9]](#cite_note-9) By October 2011, Rovio purchased Kombo, a Helsinki-based animation company.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The animation studio was acquired to produce a series of short videos released in 2012.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In March 2012, Rovio acquired Futuremark Game Studios, the game development division of [benchmarking](/wiki/Benchmark_(computing) "Benchmark (computing)") company [Futuremark](/wiki/Futuremark "Futuremark"), for an undisclosed sum.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In May 2012, Rovio announced that its game series *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")* had reached its one billionth download. In July 2012, Rovio announced a distribution partnership with [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") to bring the first three *Angry Birds* titles to video game consoles and handhelds, in a collection named *[Angry Birds Trilogy](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Trilogy "Angry Birds Trilogy")*. It was released in September 2012. In November 2012, Rovio released *[Angry Birds Star Wars](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Star_Wars "Angry Birds Star Wars")*, an iteration of its popular game licensed from the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* [original trilogy](/wiki/Star_Wars_original_trilogy "Star Wars original trilogy"), for mobile devices and PC. Rovio partnered with Activision again to port the title to video game consoles and handhelds, with it being released on those platforms in October 2013. A sequel, *[Angry Birds Star Wars II](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Star_Wars_II "Angry Birds Star Wars II")*, based on the [*Star Wars* prequel trilogy](/wiki/Star_Wars_prequel_trilogy "Star Wars prequel trilogy"), was released in September 2013.

In March 2013, Rovio launched its multi-platform [Toons.TV](/wiki/Toons.TV "Toons.TV") channel starting with *[Angry Birds Toons](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Toons "Angry Birds Toons")*.[[13]](#cite_note-13) As of 2013, Rovio became a video game publisher and is publishing third party games through its Rovio Stars program.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The channel was discontinued in 2017.

In January 2014, Rovio announced that its game series *Angry Birds* had reached its two billionth download.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In addition, it was revealed that their flagship series, *Angry Birds*, "leaked data" to third-party companies, possibly to surveillance agencies like the [NSA](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency").[[16]](#cite_note-16) In retaliation, anti-NSA hackers defaced Rovio's website.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In May 2014, Rovio launched a new publishing arm, Rovio LVL11, to release experimental games.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The first game published under Rovio LVL11 is *[Retry](/wiki/Retry_(video_game) "Retry (video game)")*[[19]](#cite_note-19) and the second is *Selfie Slam*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) As of June 2014, Rovio considers themselves an entertainment company, not just a game company. This is reinforced by Rovio's merchandise and licensing business accounting for about half of their annual revenue of $216 million in 2013.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### 2014–present: decline, initial public offering and acquisition by Sega

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rovio_Entertainment&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2014–present: decline, initial public offering and acquisition by Sega")]

In August 2014, Rovio announced that Mikael Hed would step down as CEO in January 2015 in favour of Pekka Rantala.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Hed remained on Rovio's board and became the chairman of [Rovio Animation](/wiki/Rovio_Animation "Rovio Animation"). In December 2014, Rovio [laid off](/wiki/Layoff "Layoff") 110 employees after net profits halved in 2013 due to its recent games, *[Angry Birds Epic](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Epic "Angry Birds Epic")* and *[Angry Birds Go!](/wiki/Angry_Birds_Go! "Angry Birds Go!")*, which were not as successful as past games.[[23]](#cite_note-23) After this move, Rovio closed its [Tampere](/wiki/Tampere "Tampere") studio, moving their operations to its [Espoo](/wiki/Espoo "Espoo") location. At the end of 2014, Rovio suffered from a 73% decrease in profit, earning only €10 million. Pekka Rantala stated that the decrease is due to the poor sales of the licensed merchandise and the by-products of *Angry Birds*. He also noted that "the company are unsatisfied over the result of our licensing business".[[24]](#cite_note-24) In August 2015, Rovio laid off 260 employees worldwide after Angry Birds toy and merchandise revenue fell by 43% during 2014.[[25]](#cite_note-25) In December 2015, Rantala announced that he would step down as the CEO and would be succeeded by Kati Levoranta, former chief legal officer of Rovio, in January 2016.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On 16 January 2017, Rovio opened its new game studio in London to focus on [massively multiplayer online games](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_games "Massively multiplayer online games").[[27]](#cite_note-27) On 15 February, Rovio announced that it will be cutting at least 35 jobs as it restructures the animation division.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In March, Kaiken Entertainment, founded by former Rovio CEO Mikael Hed, acquired Rovio's [animation division](/wiki/Rovio_Animation "Rovio Animation").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Later that month Rovio reported that it has returned to profitability with a gross revenue of US$201 million with the success of *[The Angry Birds Movie](/wiki/The_Angry_Birds_Movie "The Angry Birds Movie")* and its recent video games.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In June, Kaj Hed resigned as chairman of Rovio and Mika Ihamuotila succeeded him as new chairman.[[31]](#cite_note-31) On 5 September, Rovio announced its intention to become a publicly traded company.[[32]](#cite_note-32) In October, Rovio shares were sold at [NASDAQ Helsinki](/wiki/NASDAQ_Helsinki "NASDAQ Helsinki")[[33]](#cite_note-33) and the company was valued at $1 billion.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

On 2 March 2018, Rovio announced the closure of its London studio after disappointing results.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Later on 14 November, Rovio announced that it appointed the former [Gameloft](/wiki/Gameloft "Gameloft") executive Alexandre Pelletier-Normand as executive vice president of its game business unit. He started his role on January 2, 2019.[[36]](#cite_note-36) On 30 November 2018, Rovio announced that they had fully acquired PlayRaven, the developer known for making strategy games such as *Eve: War of Ascension*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

On 3 June 2020, Rovio acquired Darkfire Games for an undisclosed sum. The subsidiary became Rovio Copenhagen.[[38]](#cite_note-38) On 21 December Rovio announced that Executive Vice President of games, Alexandre Pelletier-Normand, would take over as CEO. The change went into effect on January 1, 2021.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 2021, the [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") attorney general filed a federal lawsuit against Rovio, alleging the company illegally collected and sold private [personal data](/wiki/Personal_data "Personal data") of users under thirteen to third party advertisers.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The same year, Rovio acquired Turkish casual game developer Ruby Games.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

On 15 April 2023, [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") reported that [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") would be acquiring Rovio, with a deal close to $1 billion. Rovio had rejected an earlier acquisition bid from Israeli mobile company [Playtika](/wiki/Playtika "Playtika") for $800 million.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Two days later [Sega Sammy Holdings](/wiki/Sega_Sammy_Holdings "Sega Sammy Holdings") announced that they have made a €706 million ($776 million USD) tender offer to bid for Rovio, which closed on 17 August.[[3]](#cite_note-Reid-3) On December 7, Rovio closed down the [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") based division Studio Lumi leading to the loss of 16 jobs.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) On January 8, 2026, Rovio announced that it has merged its Angry Birds licensing team into Sega's global character licensing business.[[45]](#cite_note-45) By February 2026, Sega announced it would record a $200 million impairment write-down for Rovio during Q3[[46]](#cite_note-46) due to its inability to fully implement Rovio's Beacon technology in its own mobile titles.[[47]](#cite_note-47)
